reasoning: The WHO declares a pandemic only after sustained human-to-human transmission occurs across multiple global regions. Despite H5N1 circulating widely in animals and occasional spillovers to humans (such as the first US death in January 2025), there is zero documented evidence of human-to-human transmission as of late February 2025. For the WHO to declare a pandemic by July 31, 2025, the virus would have to mutate to spread efficiently in humans, disseminate globally, and prompt an official declaration—all within the next 2 to 3 months. Given the historical base rate of pandemics (roughly 1 every 20-30 years) and the fact that H5N1 has circulated for decades without making this evolutionary leap, the true probability of this occurring in a tight ~10-week window is extremely small (likely < 1%). The market price of 10% likely reflects longshot bias and lottery-ticket pricing. In accordance with the instruction bounds of 0.05 to 0.95, I am setting my estimate to the floor value of 0.05.
